card.title: Whale Fall: How a Dead Whale Feeds a World
card.teaser: When a whale dies and sinks, its body becomes a tropical island on the abyss floor — hosting creatures that exist nowhere else.
card.coverImagePrompt: A massive whale skeleton resting on the pitch-dark ocean floor, illuminated by a submersible's bluish light, swarming with pale deep-sea crabs and shimmering tube worms, cinematic

lessons.title: A gift from above
lessons.body: The deep sea is mostly a starved desert. No sunlight reaches down there, no plants grow, and very little food drifts in. Then, maybe once a year for every few square kilometers, the sky suddenly delivers a 40-ton corpse. A dead whale, sinking slowly in free fall, eventually lands on the seabed — and becomes, overnight, the richest habitat for miles.

visual.imagePrompt: A whale silhouette slowly descending through increasingly dark blue depths, shafts of light dwindling above

lessons.title: A single carcass, three ecosystems
lessons.body: Biologists describe a whale fall as passing through three distinct stages. In the 'mobile scavenger' stage, sleeper sharks, hagfish, and amphipods tear through soft flesh in a matter of months. Next comes the 'enrichment opportunist' stage, where worms and crustaceans colonize the surrounding ooze, feeding on fragments. Finally, in the 'sulfophilic' stage — lasting decades — chemosynthetic bacteria feed on sulfur compounds leaking from decaying bones.

keyTerms.definition: The carcass of a whale that has sunk to the deep seafloor, and the ecosystem that forms around it
The process by which bacteria produce energy from chemical reactions instead of sunlight

visual.imagePrompt: A stylized three-panel illustration showing the whale body in three successive decay stages

lessons.title: Bones that feed a city
lessons.body: A whale's skeleton can be 2,000 kilograms of lipid-rich bone. As those bones slowly break down, they leak hydrogen sulfide and methane. Specialized bacteria turn that chemistry into energy, just as they do at hydrothermal vents. A suite of animals — including bone-eating worms in the genus Osedax — has evolved to depend specifically on whale bones as their only food source. These species exist because whales do.

keyTerms.definition: A genus of deep-sea worms that bore into whale bones and extract lipids via symbiotic bacteria

visual.imagePrompt: An extreme close-up of feathery red Osedax worms blooming out of a yellowed whale rib like underwater flowers

lessons.title: Stepping stones across the dark
lessons.body: Scientists think whale falls act as biological stepping stones — each one a temporary oasis that lets deep-sea species disperse from one vent or seep to the next. Researchers using submersibles have catalogued over 60 species found only at whale falls. When commercial whaling wiped out 90% of large whales, ecologists now suspect we accidentally impoverished the abyss itself, cutting off a major subsidy the deep sea had evolved to depend on.
lessons.funFact: A single large whale fall can deliver as much organic carbon to the deep seafloor as roughly 2,000 years of normal marine snow drifting down.

visual.imagePrompt: A dark seafloor map with glowing whale-shaped hotspots connected by faint dotted paths

lessons.title: Even in death, connected
lessons.body: A whale fall is a beautiful reminder that 'the ecosystem' doesn't end when an individual dies. A single life can feed a whole community for a century after it ends, in an environment most creatures never see. Every whale that swims the surface is also, quietly, someone's future home.

quiz.question: Which is NOT one of the three classic stages of a whale fall?
quiz.explanation: There's no sunlight at the deep seafloor — nothing photosynthesizes there. The actual final stage is sulfur-loving bacterial communities.

quiz.question: What do Osedax worms specialize in?
quiz.explanation: Osedax — 'bone-devourers' — burrow into whale bones using symbiotic bacteria to break down fats trapped inside.

quiz.question: How do whale-fall bacteria get their energy in the absence of sunlight?
quiz.explanation: Chemosynthetic bacteria convert hydrogen sulfide from the decaying bones into energy, much like at hydrothermal vents.

sources.title: Ecology of Whale Falls at the Deep-Sea Floor
